turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This case presents the question of whether it is proper to dismiss a criminal indictment as a sanction for mis­ conduct by government agents that did not affect the pending criminal prosecution. The case is here on writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit. The facts and simple and essentially undisputed. In April 1977, Respondent sold approximately one ounce of heroin to a government informant. In June, 1978, she was indicted for that offense and for another similar sale that she allegedly made to the same person in May of '77. The two-count indictment was returned in the District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania. Sometime before she was indicted, Respondent retained her present attorney. He has continued to represent her throughout these proceedings.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: I think the trial date was set for about -- around the middle of September. The agents told Respondent that she faced a lengthy jail sentence on the charges against her and that if she cooperated, they would make that fact known to the U.S. Attorney. They apparently represented that by cooperating, she would improve her chances for a favor­ able sentence. They also advised her of the Witness Protection Plan that the government had available. Agent Hopson inquired whether Respondent was represented by retained counsel or whether she was represented by a public defender.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does the record show whether the United States Attorney was aware of these visits?

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: I think the record shows that the United States Attorney was not aware of these visits, Mr. Chief Justice. There was a contact between Respondent's attorney and the Assistant United States Attorney responsible for the case before the indictment was ever returned, but there's nothing in the record to suggest that the Assistant U.S. Attorney knew that the agents were going to visit Respondent.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Didn't the agents also tell her that this judge before whom she was scheduled to be tried was notoriously tough, or --

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: The Respondent testified at the hearing that the agents did refer to the District judge as a tough whether he had made any soecific comments about the judge personally, but that he certainly had told her that she faced a severe sentence. With respect to any of these discrepancies in the testimony, Mr. Justice Stewart, there is a footnote right at then he was sound on his conclusion, I suppose.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: I suppose so, Mr. Chief Justice. Respondent in any event, didn't say anything to the agents and they left; they left a phone number where they could be reached. She immediately called her attorney, he went to her house and at his instruction and in his presence she called Agent Hopson and said she'd like to arrange a meeting for the returned several hours later and the Respondent was home by herself, or she said she had company and therefore couldn't speak with them at the time. Agent Hopson promised to call later to set up an appointment for the following day. He did, was made to meet, but in any event the agents returned for the motion, Morrison entered an opinion-conditional plea of guilty

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: That must be a reprint, Mr. Justice Stewart. She entered a conditional plea of guilty, I don't know what the word opinion is doing there.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: She entered a conditional plea of guilty?

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Lefkowitz v. Newsome too, was a case reporter for the criminal rule.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let's assume there's a plea of guilty that isn't conditional. Is it appealable? guilty was not conditioned, this -- denial of this motion would not be appealable, no.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why? I mean, why isn't the plea of guilty appealable? The judgment is entered on the plea.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well ordinarily --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why isn't it appealable?

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Ordinarily, Mr. Justice White, the rule is that the plea of guilty waives any other objections to the admissibility of evidence --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well what about the McCarthy case, the I'm not sure that an evidentiary matter would be one of those, this kind of situation perhaps falls somewhere in between the constitutionality of the statute on which the person has been convicted or just the matter of suppression of evidence. In any event, that was the procedure that was adopted and the District Court thereafter conducted a hearing on Respon dent's motion to dismiss and then he denied it, the District judge denied it without rendering any opinion. Respondent pled guilty to Count I, and at the Rule 11 proceeding that mant in April 1977. She then appealed the denial of her motion to dismiss, in accordance with the agreement; the Court of Appeals reversed. The Court acknowledged that Respondent did not provide any information to the DEA.. The Court said that the importuning of the government agents was unsuccessful. The Court didn't suggest that the agents' actions had damaged that this dismissal was the only sanction available that would vanced two alternative arguments that the Court of Appeals decision is incorrect. They are parallel in many respects. First, we've argued that the DBA agents' actions did not vio­ secution was not affected, and some discernible prejudice is an essential element with a Sixth Amendment violation. Second, we’ve said that even if the agents' conduct did infringe Respondent's constitutional right, dismissal of the indictment is not an appropriate remedy here because the violation caused Respondent no injury.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Does the government disagree with Judge Garth's statement dissenting -- granting en banc consideration at page 21(a) where he says "the panel's analysis reduces to the following non sequitur since the DEA agents' conduct was the essence of the government's second argument, that regard­ less of whether or not this conduct is thought to violate Respondent's Sixth Amendment rights in some abstract sense, to dismiss the indictment in this case where there's been no prejudice whatsoever, is to essentially reverse all the rules of relief and remedy that have been developed through the years, because the more effective --- I mean, the more drastic relief is given in the case where the harm is the least serious, in fact, non-existent. Before we even get to that point, however, the rights weren't violated at all. The Sixth Amendment provides that in all criminal prosecutions, the accused shall enjoy the right to have the assistance of counsel for his defense. Resondent had the assistance of counsel for her defense at all stages of the prosecution. She doesn't suggest that her counsel's assistance was in any way impaired and therefore the judice alleged --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's all she says. She just says these things happened and she says, "P.S. there was no intereference with a fair trial."

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: That's right, Mr. Justice White. The burden of this first argument is that no constitutional viola­ tion has occurred. Again, that's not to say that this was right, it's just to say that the constitutional rights haven't been

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Yes. conduct.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well that's essentially what the Court--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even though it didn't violate any pro­ vision of the Constitution.

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: That's what the Court of Appeals' panel where we're trying to decide what the power of the federal courts is, to dismiss criminal charges in the absence of any constitutional or statutory violation. And that's -- at least, as the case comes to this Court that situation is not presentee here .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well this action on the part of the Third Circuit has to be justified under supervisory power, doesn't it?

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well, I don't believe so, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I'm not saying it is justified, it must be justified in order to have it stand. That's -- isn't that your position?

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well, it depends on how you use the phrase supervisory power. The Court of Appeals said that this action was necessary in order to vindicate Respondent's rights under the Sixth Amendment. It rested the decision on the constitutional provision. As I understand supervisory power, i t ordinarily is a phrase used in cases where no statutory or constitutional violation is involUed but the Court neverthe­ less desire to adjust certain conduct that it believes is in­ appropriate for one reason or another. used to dismiss indictments when --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well that's something else again. MR.BUSCEMI: Exactly. QUESTION; But the federal Appellate Courts have a supervisory power, called by whatever name, which they would not have over state courts, is that not so?

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Right. That's correct, that's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that there is something in a super­ visory category that is different and distinctive from con­ stitutional?

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Yes, but --- given by Congress to any litigant who loses in the District Court to appeal to the Court of Appeals, and to anyone who loses in the Court of Appeals to petition for certiorari here, do they not? I mean --

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well that's right. I mean, there's no question that the Court's supervisory power is dependent upon the acquiescence of Congress. I mean, this Court has recognized that in the absence of any -- if Congress decides to change this Court's supervisory power in some way, it can brief last term in the Japan case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So what you're really saying is that there's no constitutional violation as long as all -- that this was just an attempt? MR.BUSCEMI: That's the burden of the first argument, is there's an attempt to violate the Constitution but it didn't succeed and so there's no violation. Now --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that even if there is -- even if it's a violation, you say the remedy is just excessive, and I suppose

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I suppose that the fact of violation would be not open to relitigation, in a civil suit?

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Well, I don't see how there could be a civil suit, because as part of the terms of the settlement there's been a waiver of all future claims against the govern­ ment or its agents as a result of the conduct that's involved in this case. Now, I think I'd like to turn to the government's second argument. The only other point that I thought I'd make on the first argument, to refer the Court to Massiah, which I think is instructive because of the way in which the Court decided the case focusing on the use of the evidence at trial rather than the way in which the evidence was obtained. I recommend -- I commend that part of Massiah to the Court's attention, I think we quoted from it at some length in the brief. Now, I think that in this respect the Court of Appeals'main error is that it assumed that whatever remedy there could be had to be a remedy in this criminal proceeding. This criminal proceeding involves events that occurred 16 The agent's visit was wrong, but it didn't affect that pro­ ceeding and there's no reason to think that any relief awarded has to be awarded in the pending criminal proceeding.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: ’ There has been a civil recovery, I think there's a reference to that --

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: Yes, there's been -- the reply brief describes the civil recovery at the very beginning, I think, on pages 1 and 2. Respondent received, I believe, $4500, 25% of which was to be paid to -- was designated for attorneys fees. to that, or was that --

turns.name: PETER BUSCEMI
turns.text: The attorney in the criminal case filed an independent lawsuit against the DEA agents, charging them Respondent and he did recover $2,000, again, 25% of that to be paid to his attorney in the civil suit. Now, I'd like to conclude by addressing myself for a moment to the Court of Appeals' asserted need for violation I think that this civil recovery is very significant, Finally, even if -- we've cited some Court of Appeals cases in our brief that generally support the government's position here, but leave open the possibility that dismissal of an indictment may be permissible if the Court finds itself counsel for the defense. I have not been planted here by the government because if that really did occur, if the government attempts were successful, then I wouldn't be here. And to a very large degree, there would be no other adversary system like we have now. I am here in a sense, not only representing Hazel Morrison, I am representing the adversary system of which we all are members. To allow this behavior to go un­ checked when there are known attempts permits the government to do away with their adversary. In the trial courtroom, to that extent, it is a violation of the Sixth Amendment; of

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I thought the Court of Appeals would refer you to the remedy for this person, not a broad- scale remedy for society?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So the $4500 was the remedy for what the Court of Appeals thought that no remedy existed.

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, I thought that the remedy as far as the system was concerned, and as far as Hazel was concerned, was the dismissal of the prosecution and also, together with that dismissal, it was an additional remedy. It was not an either/or, because if it becomes .aneither/or then As I said, you will allow the government to make these attempts -- if they are successful we will never know about it; you'll have litigants passing in front of trial judges, and he'll never know who represents which side. But now, when we've discovered --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm not sure I follow that argument. Well, if he'd been successful she would have fired you and gotten the public defender.

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: And -- the public defenders are faithless to their clients, are yon?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: Well you know, the government agents, although the factual rendition was given to you by --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well are you implying that? Are you --

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: I don't agree with that as the facts.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Pardon me? MR.CUCINOTTA: I don't agree with that as the facts. Those are not the facts. She was only told she could and when she asked for her right to counsel, she was told that she would face a harsher jail sentence and she stood with five years as the first conviction. To this day, Hazel Morrison and the public, more importantly, does not know whether or not indeed the agents really were successful in their threat. And that is the analysis which --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What are you talking about?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's what I don't follow. What are you saying, what would have happened if the worst had happened?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: If the worst would have happened, is exemplified, is lying --- you'd have to go on and imagine -- use your imagination, as exemplified in cases like People v. Moore and People v. Mora, out of California. There it was found, a reported situation where an individual went along with the government overtures, dismissed his counsel j QUESTION: Well try.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Try and find something that you were

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: There is a taint -- ment's argument regarding the lack of prejudice --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, I'm not --- I'm not referring to the government's argument at all. I'm talking about what I'm asking you. You say that you -- something the government did deprived this appellant, this Respondent, of a fair trial and association with counsel.

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now what?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: To rely on the -- on the confiden­ tiality of our communications, to not --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How would that hurt her?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: They didn't succeed. Because if they succeeded, I would not be here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what did you get this $4500 for?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: The $4500 was an additional remedy, Your Honor, I suggest that Hazel Morrison's right to an ad­ versary, due process hearing is worth $4500.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Were you intimidated in any way?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: Oh yes, Your Honor, I've been intimi­ dated for several years, in a sense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In this -- this case?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: In this case. which way --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you'd know whether you are intimi­ dated or not, don't you? Don't you?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: All I have to do, Your Honor, is read that they can come into Court with search warrants and accuse me of criminal activity, tell me to go get a lawyer, in a adversary proceeding --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was that this case?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. And that intimidated you?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: It sure did, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: When I have a federal court which I respect telling me, threatening me with possible contempt and disciplinary action --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You know --

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: -- because all I was trying to do was prove the truth, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You know you're not guilty, don't you? You know, I'm looking at you and to me, you don't appear to be

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's right, I thought so. I thought so.

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: Your Honor, I believe that once you find the government involved in this kind of activity, which is the most egregious kind of record that this Court has ever seen, is not lying on search warrants, it is not lying to the Grand Jury, it is not -- and in fact, you've got some lies in this particular case. You've got them lying on the search warrant, you've got them lying at least on three occas­ ions in their answer to my motion to dismiss, when they said tell her anything about a sentence, et cetera. And then they came into Court and they really hedged and hawed, until finally they realized that possibly they had to decimate, an attempt to terminate an individuals' right to counsel, and in that sense, we're talking about terminating all of our right to counsel, because if you let it go here are punitive and compensatory damages requested. I can't go any further than that, Mr. Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And they were ultimately settled?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: Yes, and I -- urge the Court to find that whether they were settled, whether the right exists and It's merely an extra form of relief. If I felt in any way that exchanging certain amounts of money would be selling out the adversary system, I'd feel sort of like Judas, giving up Christ. Because without him it would be all over. In the -- in fact, in the release -- I can tell the Court that in the release, there's a statement that says that this release has nothing to do with the criminal case But that's getting out of the record, so once again I suggest to the Court, that's not part of the record.

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: The civil -- the civil relief, the civil case. It was not only -- Via v. Cliff, said that the civil remedy was an extra remedy not an alternative remedy--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, they are a matter of public record, though?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: Oh yes, yes. What I'm saying, I'm -- in this case in the Court's record down in the Clerk's Office, you're not going to find the papers associated with the civil suit. claim adjusters, for example, go to a plaintiff in a personal injury suit without telling counsel and make the same kind of slanderous remarks about counsel. Has there ever been a suggestion that the appropriate remedy in that case is to make some kind of a ruling on the merits in the litigation?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: I’m not familiar with any --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Since it's exactly the same threat to the adversary system.

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: Particular cases like that, Mr. sions with my colleagues in the civil bar. Perhaps the time has come --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Maybe by analogy what you should do is

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: You can get into all sorts of specu­ lations. I sometimes speculate about how, what would happen if Hazel went to Steve Hopson and said if you don’t get a new prosecutor who is a little more easy on me, then you are not going to see your kids anymore. So, -- but one of the points I want to raise with the Court is that when you see this kind of activity in a record, that it's up to the government to

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought the law was the other way? MR.CUCINOTTA: No, it's not the burden on the defendant, or the defense counsel, to show that the item has been unconstitutionally acquired. But be that as it may, Mr. Justice, it is when you have situations such as this, which are really violations of due process, you don't want to get involved in what I call nice calculations as to the extent of the denial, of the right to counsel. It's up to them, to suddenly turn around and say well, it hasn't been denied for this, this and this reason. Well, they haven't said that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, whatever burden -- whoever has got two things: either there wasn't any prejudice whatsoever, or that whether there is or not, whether there is or not the indictment should be dismissed. Which did the Court say, no -- there is no prejudice?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: I think they said both. They said there has been an infringement --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well then, whoever had the burden carried it?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: In terms of the motion to dismiss

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If the government had the burden, they must have carried it, because the Court concluded that there wasn't any prejudice.

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: If the Court please, I suggest in terms of the motion to dismiss the indictment, the Respondent did carry the burden, he did prove his case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Since you put this so heavily in your argument, on exposition and defense of the adversary system, what if this Court had said the next five drug cases are all ment? Would that be an excessive remedy? my client, that he was going to be able to get her, not -- the U.S. Attorney wasn't going to do it, the judge wasn't going to do it. You can't wait for the next time. There may be no next time, Mr. Chief Justice, and if you wait for the next five cases you may not see them. But I think they're already there. And if there's 500 cases like this, and suddenly you get the floodgates argument -- like I saw in their petition for cert, then please bring the floodgates on, because doesn't this earlier, then there really is no problem.

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: The Court dismisses the indictment, there will be no next time. There may have been past occas­ ions that the activity has occurred and people are languishing in prison who were afraid to raise this issue because they struck a deal, told their lawyer to go- -- to go somewhere else, who couldn't raise it because they couldn't prove it. Or, were afraid that they would be sentenced harsher if the Court thought that they were lying. And look what happened in my case. We had -- we tried --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You know, there are actually some inno­ cent people in jail. So I don't know what all this argument's about.

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: Your Honor, that's what I'm suggestin

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you suggest to the Court that because something happened in some case 80 years ago, in the state of

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: Yes sir. I respectfully submit to this Court --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Somewhere, will you -- if you've done it, I missed it, will you give me your answer to Judge Garth's observation in his dissent and denial of the rehearing en banc, that your position is such that when there is prejudice such as by the admission of illegal evidence, there is a new trial. But here where there is no prejudice, that the indictment was dismissed, how do those things square?

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: I separate in my mind the concept -- as the Fourth Amendment applies to real physical evidence. facts of Morrisey v. Brewer, but I will -- I do know that there's no other case that involved disparaging counsel of a person's particular free choice, that it would be private counsel that makes it even worse. So to that extent, then, the: remedy in that particular case is different and distinguished from the kind of relief I'm asking here, in terms of what has occurred, the -- counsel had no way of determining the validity of those offers of negotiation that were made to my client, when they said to my client, we can give you X-Y-Z if you do A, B and C. If -- there was a deal that was struck, how could sentenced harshly, which has been indicated in the reply brief that these overtures by the government were actually genuine, uses the word genuine on page 3, says there was nothing in the record to show that the overtures of the govern­ ment agents were actually anything but genuine, Indeed they must have been genuine, but we today, don't know whether or not she was sentenced so harshly because of what the agents said

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What was the sentence? I didn't --

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: Five years, to a girl who had never been convicted of a crime before, and ---

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: For some of them it’s a five-year minimum

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mandatory.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Some have -- are you --

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: I’m not familiar with that, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I think he might know.

turns.name: SALVATORE J. CUCINOTTA
turns.text: Now I also believe that in this case you will find fear in the Petitioner of any further communications with counsel of her choice. She --there are third meeting, that Russ was coming. And Russ was the investi­ gator. And the person who really showed up was not Russ, but it was the DEA agents. This is a case that’s replete with Court please, this is a case that does not involve the kinds of lies that you've seen the government get involved in, in the past, in terms of some of these other cases. They are just misstatements of the truth, you're involved in what I con­ sider extreme serious lack of understanding as to the require­ ments of the Sixth Amendment. If the Court please, my client pled guilty to the North American Reporting hereby certifies that the attached pages represent an accurate transcript of electronic sound recording of the oral argument before the Supreme Court of the United States in the matter of: No. 79-395 UNITED STATES OF AMERICA, v. HAZEL MORRISON and that these pages constitute the original transcript of the proceedings for the records of the Court. bn <
